observations.recommendation:
1.7 The Audit Team recommended that the LCE require the Municipal
Accountant to prepare the corresponding JEV to adjust and record the
long outstanding checks by debiting Cash in Bank - LCCA account and
crediting Accounts Payable account in consonance with Section 59 of the
MNGAS for LGUs, hence, cash in bank balance is accurately presented
in the financial statements.

2.9 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the
MSWDO and MAO and those in-charge in the distribution of various
items together with the Supply Officer to submit immediately to the
Municipal Accountant the duly accomplished distribution list and any
other required supporting documents substantiating the
distribution/utilization or issuance of inventory items to the end-users.
Hence, the distributed goods will be recorded as expenses by Municipal
Accountant. This is in consonance with Sections 114 and 121, Volume 1
of the MNGAS for LGUs, thus the affected accounts will be fairly
presented in the financial statements.

3.8 The Audit Team recommended that the LCE direct the Inventory
Committee to conduct physical count of its PPE and require the Property
Officer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile the results of the physical
count with Accounting records and submit immediately a copy of
RPCPPE to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year as
mandated in the aforementioned Section 124, Volume I of the MNGAS
for LGUs. Furthermore, take advantage of the purposes of COA
Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 by following its guidelines
and procedures in order to reflect a reliable and accurate balances of
PPE accounts, hence, fairly presented in the FS and convey precise
information useful in decision making and accountability for these
properties.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer to require all revenue collectors to accomplish the RCDs at the close of each business day and remit the collections to the Liquidating Officer on a daily basis pursuant to Section 29 of the MNGAS for LGUs. Further, a cut-off time may be designated by the Municipal Treasurer for which remittance should be made on a daily basis and intact, and collections after the cut-off time shall be remitted the following working day.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Consider designating a team or employees within the Municipal Treasury Office through a memorandum. This team will be responsible for preparing, maintaining, and updating the certified list of taxpayers, which should include their names, property descriptions, periods of arrears, and the amounts due based on the Real Property Tax Account Register and the taxpayer’s index card.
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the certified list of taxpayers. This will ensure an accurate and reliable basis for establishing receivables for Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special Education Tax (SET) at the beginning of the year, thus guaranteeing the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
The Municipal Accountant to discontinue the practice of closing the previous years’ RPT and SET receivables balance to accurately reflect the actual amounts owed by taxpayers in the financial statements.
The Treasury Office to:
Continue the information drive and tax campaign to raise constituents' awareness about the advance payment of real property taxes and encourage them to take advantage of discounts, promoting timely payments and enhancing the Municipality's collection efficiency on RPT/SET; and
Continue sending letters to delinquent taxpayers and post a notice of delinquency at the main entrance of the municipal hall, as well as in a publicly accessible and visible location in each Barangay of the local government unit involved. Additionally, the Municipality may utilize available legal remedies to generate more revenue that can be allocated for the implementation of more programs and projects for the benefit of its constituents.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to:
For CY 2024, purchased Semi-expendable recorded as PPE, prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify semi-expendable property that was incorrectly recorded under the Property, Plant and Equipment as follows:
Issued to end-users
On the prior years purchased Semi-expendable recorded to PPE accounts which still have remaining useful life, prepare the following necessary adjusting entries, to wit:
Issued to end-users
On the prior years purchased Semi-expendable recorded to PPE accounts without remaining useful life, prepare the following necessary adjusting entries, which are:
To enhance accountability and internal control over semi-expendable property, the Municipal Government to ensure the proper preparation and maintenance of the following prescribed forms, registries, and reports, as applicable, pursuant to Item 4.7 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022:
Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card (SPLC)
Registry of Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RegSPI)
Receipt of Returned Semi-Expendable Property (RRSP)
Report of Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RSPI)
Report on the Physical Count of Semi-Expendable Property (RPCSP)
Report of Lost, Stolen, Damaged, or Destroyed Semi-Expendable Property (RLSDDSP)
Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Semi-Expendable Property (IIRUSP)
These measures will ensure proper recording, monitoring, and accountability over semi-expendable property, reducing the risk of loss, misuse, or misstatement in the Municipality’s financial reports.

We recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 be complied with.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
The Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network; and
The Municipal GSO, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keeps a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and their components.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Provide loan amortization of the loans acquired by the Municipality. Disclose loan amortization details, such as, but not limited to: (a) schedule of payment; (b) principal amount due; (c) interest due; and (d) carrying amount under Note 11 of the Notes to Financial Statements.
Reclassify the Loans Payable – Domestic in the Statement of Financial Position and Notes to Financial Statements. (Refer to Table 3)
Current portion – 	        ₱     17,222,907.16
Non-current portion –         104,259,817.58
Prepare an adjusting journal entry to recognize the accrual of interest expense as follows:
Interest Expense/Prior Year Adjustments	₱461,607.93
Interest Payable					₱461,607.93
To recognize the accrual of interest expenses on loans for CY 2024

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare an adjusting journal entry to correct the erroneous entry in the General Fund during transfer of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF, as follows:
Provide the details of the breakdown of Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund as disclosures in the Note to Financial Statements, as required by IPSAS 1.
Management to adhere to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 mandating LGUs that at year-end all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) and the Disaster Risk Management Fund for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (DRRMF-MOOE) shall be transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the account "Trust Liability-DRRM" (Code 2-04-01-020) in the Trust Fund Books.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to provide the necessary disclosures and comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS and the Manual on NGAS for LGUs by adequately inserting a concise narrative on significant changes and a breakdown of material balances to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the Financial Statements. The accountant should also trace the details of the differences arising from the reconciliation of SCBAA and SFPer under MOOE and Personal Services.
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer must maintain RAAOs to support the actual amounts in the SCBAA and reconcile these amounts with those presented in the SFPer.

We have recommended that Management:
Direct the GAD M&E Team to evaluate the outcomes of the LGU's GAD policies, programs, and projects. This evaluation should result in a GAD Evaluation Report, which must be submitted to the DILG Regional Office by the end of the three-year term (2022-2025). This report will serve as valuable input for local policymaking and planning, including GAD planning, programming, budgeting, and the implementation of the current administration's initiatives.
Additionally, ensure that an updated GAD Database is maintained to facilitate the identification of gender issues and guarantee the full implementation of the Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) outlined in the GAD Plan and Budget.
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with the LGU GFPS-TWG Chairman, to avoid charging expenses that are not related to addressing gender-related issues. Additionally, the PPAs that do not address gender-related concerns due to ambiguity should be charged to MOOE instead of the fund designated for GAD.

1.11 We recommended and Management agreed to take the following courses of
action:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to record and include in the Trust Fund
books and financial statements the balances of the seven (7) Cash in
Bank-LCCA and the corresponding liability accounts, to ensure the
accurate presentation of the balance of the Cash in Bank account; and

b. Direct the Accounting unit to identify and examine the long outstanding
stale checks and to record the necessary adjusting entries in the books.

2.12 We recommended and Management agreed to observe the guidelines and
procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020,
for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to ensure the reliability
and verifiability of PPE balances in terms of existence, condition, and
accountability, as follows:

a. Adopt the uniform numbering system for PPE and require the
Property Unit to update the property stickers based on the prescribed
format and to include the required information under Section 5.6 and
5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;

b. Temporarily relieve the members of the Inventory Committee from
their regular duties to allow them to fully devote their time and
attention to the conduct of the physical inventory taking; and

c. Strictly comply with the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of
physical count of PPE, recognition of PPE items found at the station,
and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items. Additionally,
require the Municipal Accountant and Property Officer to reconcile
their records based on the results of the actual physical inventory and
make the necessary adjustments accordingly.

3.18 We recommended and Management agreed to strictly adhere to the
accounting and reporting guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2015-008
by taking the following actions:

a. The Inventory Committee conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of
all local road networks within the Municipality's jurisdiction and
reconcile the inventory with the records maintained by the accounting
office, if available.

b. The Municipal Engineer collaborate with the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to provide a complete description and cost
breakdown of road components for all local road networks, coordinating
with the Municipal Assessor to accurately assess the road lot components.

c. The Municipal Treasurer establish and maintain a Local Road Inventory,
Road Map, and Local Road Network Property Card for each road
network, documenting relevant information.

d. The Municipal Accountant ensure that all local road networks and their
respective components are recorded in the municipality's books of
accounts, with depreciation expenses for each component also be
accounted for.

e. The Municipal Accountant include a disclosure of the total road network
system in the Notes to the Financial Statements of the Municipality.

4.9 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to submit a written explanation, along
with complete supporting documentation, for the discrepancies noted
between the audit-reconstructed and submitted Trial Balances for CY
2024.

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with Sections 4(e)
and 70, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs by maintaining complete
General and Subsidiary Ledgers for all control accounts (e.g., Cash,
Receivables, Inventories, Property, Plant and Equipment, Liabilities,
Income, and Expenses), and ensure that these records are submitted to the
COA Auditor and made available for audit.

c. Ensure that accounting responsibilities are assigned to qualified
accountable personnel, and that supervisory controls are strengthened to
maintain the integrity, accuracy, and reliability of financial records.

5.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the timely
liquidation of cash advances by the accountable officers concerned in
accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002, and strengthen internal controls
over the granting, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.

5.7 We further recommended that Management exhaust all possible remedies
and demand the immediate liquidation of the foregoing unliquidated cash
advances.

6.6 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a) Prepare and record a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reclassify the
amount recorded under Advances to Contractors to Construction in
Progress;

b) Recognize and record the CIP for ongoing projects to ensure accurate
presentation of the CIP account balance in the financial statements; and

c) Coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in monitoring partially completed
infrastructure projects to ensure timely and proper recognition of the CIP
account.

7.8 We recommended and Management agreed to:

a) Demand the immediate refund of the unrecouped advances and forfeit
the corresponding irrevocable standby letters of credit or
guarantee/surety bonds posted by the contractor.

b) Initiate legal proceedings should the contractor refuse to reimburse the
unrecovered advances, if deemed necessary; and

c) Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of existing issuances
regarding the recoupment of advance payments made to contractors.

8.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the appropriate
adjusting entry to capitalize the borrowing cost incurred in the construction of
the qualifying asset, in accordance with the requirements of IPSAS 5, and
ensure consistency in recording borrowing cost to present an accurate
balance of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) and interest expense.

loss due to theft or
misappropriation.

observations.recommendation: 1. We further recommended that the wages of laborers performing regular repair and maintenance work be charged to the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in endorsing specific PPAs to the SB for approval, indicate the project location, specific implementation schedule, and physical target to facilitate the timely implementation of the PPAs. For appropriations already presented in a lump sum or in generic terms, a separate SB appropriation ordinance or resolution shall be secured by the Local Chief Executive before utilizing the fund. Henceforth, Management is enjoined to strictly comply with Article 454(d) of the IRR of R.A. No. 7160.

observations.recommendation: Immediately organize an Internal Audit Service (IAS) to comply with Section 4 of AO No. 70.

1.7 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO, Municipal Agriculturist and all Municipal Employees who were in-charge in the distribution of various items together with the Supply Officer to submit immediately to the Municipal Accountant the duly accomplished distribution lists and other required supporting documents substantiating the utilization or issuance of  inventory items to the end-users as her basis in recording the items under the expense account. Thus, the affected accounts will be fairly presented in the FS.

2.7 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of LGU’s PPE and submit immediately the required RPCPPE to the AT not later than January 31 of each year as mandated in Section 124, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs. Furthermore, it was recommended that management strictly observe compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to reflect a reliable and accurate balances of PPE accounts, hence to convey precise information useful in decision making and accountability for these properties. We also recommended that Management prioritize the reconciliation of the results of the physical count with the accounting records for the fair presentation of the PPE in the FS.

3.5 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor oblige the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to require all collectors to turn over to her their collections at the end of each day. Moreover, require also the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact daily or on the next banking day all collections turned over to her including her collections in order not to expose such collections to a possible risk of loss due to theft or misappropriation.

4.8 	We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct final review on the existence of records and subsidiary ledgers of the Liability accounts, after which revert to the surplus of the GF those accounts which have no actual claim, administrative or judicial, has been filed or which is not covered by perfected contracts on record, to fairly present the balance of the specific accounts in the FS, in accordance with Section 98 of the PD 1445. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the requirements of Section 59 of the same PD thus, recognize and record accurately only the duly supported and bonafide or genuine obligations of the municipality.

5.6 	We recommended and management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to withhold taxes based on the gross amounts of contracts as mandated in the aforementioned BIR regulations. Likewise, for future transactions of the same nature, strictly adhere to the requirements of the BIR so as to withhold the correct amount of tax to have additional funds for programs and projects of the national government for the whole citizenry.

6.6 	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to approve employees’ loan applications only within their capacity to pay in order to meet the minimum net take home pay as required under Section 56 of General Appropriations Act of 2023, thus the employees could possibly meet the basic family needs before the next payroll period which could motivate them to work effectively and efficiently.  Also, the government will not act as collecting agent of the private lending institutions, hence the additional expense of the government will be avoided.

7.6 	We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and the BAC to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents and justifications on the deficiencies observed for further evaluation of the transactions and to avoid suspension in audit. Also, oblige the Municipal Accountant to see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with the required basic documents to ensure validity and propriety of the transactions.

8.4 	We recommended that management require the Municipal Engineer and MPDC to meticulously and judiciously plan various infrastructure projects of the municipality before its implementation in accordance with the Revised IRR of RA 9184 to avoid issuance of multiple SWO. Thus, the projects are completed as per target completion date and immediately serve the purpose for which these were planned, funded and implemented.

9.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to stop paying supplies and materials in advance to avoid possible loss of government funds in case the items paid are not delivered and to enable the withholding of corresponding penalty in case of late delivery and undelivered items as per contract/purchase order.  We further recommended to strictly observe Section 88 of PD 1445 in the procurement of goods and services to avoid possible suspension or disallowance of such transaction.

10.6 	We recommended and management agreed to cause the immediate return of the unexpended/unutilized balances enumerated in the above table to the SA. Henceforth, return all balances of fully implemented project funded from NGAs, thereby allowing the SA/NGAs to reappropriate the same for other priority projects that will benefit stakeholders and to avoid carrying out the balances and frequent preparation of the schedule of unreturned funds under the Due to NGAs account.

11.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Government procure its common-use supplies and equipment from the PS-DBM in conformity with the provisions of Sections 1 and 4 of AO No. 17 dated July 28, 2011 and do away from the tedious procurement process while availing the supplies and equipment at a cost most advantageous to the government.

12.6 	We recommended and Management agreed to require the BAC and the Supply Officer to strictly adhere to the requirements of COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, specifically item B therein. Likewise, advise the BAC to require the dealers/suppliers to indicate the brand names of the items being quoted in the RFQ which will be the basis of the BAC in filling-out of the PO and the Supplier for the issuance of Sales Invoice, thus delivered goods/items could be easily verified and validated.

13.5 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and OIC- Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the required necessary supporting documents are complete prior to the payment of the transactions. Furthermore, strictly adhere to the provisions of NIRC by requiring the dealers to religiously prepare and submit their Sales Invoices or Official Receipts depending on the nature of transaction upon receipt of payment, in order to help the national government in collecting funds for national development.
13.6 	We also recommended management to: a) limit expenditures on meals and snacks in the conduct of essential priority programs/projects/activities within the context of general welfare; and b) review the appropriations for Representation Expenses and in close coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, identify avoidable costs for meals/snacks to strategically adjust proposed budget.

15.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to furnish the AT list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year as mandated in the aforementioned COA Circular in order that the AT could conduct timely validation and monitoring of the implementation of infrastructure projects and to inform management promptly of any deficiency for the latter’s immediate and appropriate action.

16.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and furnish the same to the LDRRMO for the latter to reconcile with his record, after which submit the copy to the AT not later than the 15th day after the end of each month to enable the auditor to conduct timely review, verification and monitoring of the sources and utilization of LDRRMF, thus deficiencies if any, will be informed to management for the latter’s immediate and appropriate action thereof.

17.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain separate special accounts for market and slaughterhouse under the GF and prepare separate financial reports to provide sufficient information about the profitability, self-sustainability and operation of these that can serve as a guide to come up with better economic decisions by the management and to have ready information necessary for the interested parties and the general public. In addition, cashbooks for both collections and disbursements including separate bank accounts be maintained for the slaughterhouse to come up with an immediate financial information of the municipality’s economic enterprises.

18.8 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to see to it that all eligibility requirements are present and valid at the time of opening of bids using a nondiscretionary “pass/fail” criterion in order to ascertain that all government contracts will be awarded only to eligible contractors or suppliers.

19.5 	We recommended and Management agreed to require the concerned municipal employees to settle their cash advances on the period within which to settle cash advances as mandated in COA Circular 97-002, thus minimizing the risk of exposing government funds to possible loss or misappropriation. Moreover, we recommended Management to require the employees who have unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2023 to immediately submit their liquidation reports, otherwise implement item 5.9 of the above circular to cover for their unliquidated cash advances.

20.7 	We recommended that henceforth, Management shall see to it that dealers/suppliers/contractors comply with the stipulations stated in the contract and impost liquidated damages, or rescind or terminate the contract as prescribed in Section 68 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.  Hence, observe strict compliance with the agreement stipulated in the contract to avoid breach of contract and any possible repercussions thereof.

21.6 	We recommended that Management adhere strictly to the provisions set forth under Section 3 of the COA No. 80-101-C dated July 28, 1980 and furnish immediately certified copy of the Suspension Order to the Auditor, to enable the latter to verify and determine if the grounds for the issuance is valid, reasonable and reliable, thus a timely information of discrepancies, if any, could be communicated to management for its immediate and appropriate action and to have a basis in the computation and imposition of the corresponding liquidated damages for every day of delay, if warranted.

22.7 	We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to adopt the required procedures and conditions in the verification and validation of all documents submitted by the bidders/suppliers to ensure that the Municipal Mayor being the Head of the Procuring Entity is entering into a contract with a technically, legally and financially capable supplier to promote competitiveness in the procurement activities.

23.8 	We recommended that Management:
23.8.1 	Instruct the Municipal Engineer to conduct necessary field surveys and investigations in the detailed engineering and carefully study/evaluate the site condition in the preparation of accurate design plans, technical specifications, quantity and cost estimates and program of work for infrastructure projects to minimize any work suspension that may cause delay in the completion of the projects.
23.8.2	Require the BAC and Municipal Engineer to review all contracts pertaining to infrastructure projects to determine the target date of completion, monitor if the contract time has expired and Management implement calibrated actions provided in GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 prior to termination in part or in whole, of the work that has not been completed and accepted under a contract.
Impose administrative sanctions, if warranted, in accordance with Section 69 of the IRR of RA 9184, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent similar incurrences of extended delays in the implementation of the projects.

24.5 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor intensify the implementation of the PPAs funded out from the 20% DF and require the MPDC to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to monitor closely the implementation thereof so as to deliver to the constituent timely benefits that could be derived from these projects and to achieve a broader scope of development. We also recommended management to reappropriate/realign projects which are not implementable due to some constraints including the remaining amounts of projects already implemented. We further recommended management to plan/program projects which are attainable and implementable within the prescribed period.

26.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer initiate a systematic implementation of waste disposal in the Municipality, thus clean and healthy environment for the municipality and its constituents as a whole would be attained. We also recommended that the LCE encourage greater private sector participation in solid waste management and the possible empowerment of the barangay officials or any residents to exercise reasonable control over the improper disposal of waste for the general safety of the constituents, especially those living near the Sanitary Landfill.

27.5 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the MPDC judiciously and carefully plan and prioritize/implement the programs and projects funded out from its 20% DF. Likewise, strictly observe the requirements of Section 3.2 of DBM – DOF and DILG JMC to prevent wastage of government funds.

29.5 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Point System Officer to intensify and monitor the implementation of planned GAD activities in    order to address a broader scope of gender issues and concerns that is, to promote women empowerment and gender equality in the locality and to the country as a whole.

For the Municipal Government to have the assurance that inventories are safeguarded against the risk of misapplication or misuse, we reiterated the prior year's recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the concerned municipal officials to:
Record inventory using the prescribed perpetual method of accounting on all purchased supplies and materials and use inventory tags or stock cards for monitoring; and
Require the Inventory Committee and the Office of the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain complete SCs and SLCs, respectively, and reconcile their records regularly to ensure that all purchases and issuances are properly accounted for.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical count of all types of inventory items semestrally, prepare the RPCI and submit the same to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively. Also, invite representative from the COA to act as witness in the physical count; and
Prepare adjusting journal entry to revert the Drugs and Medicines Inventory received on January 2024 but already recognized in 2023, as follows:
Accounts Payable			₱2,798,430.00
Drugs and Medicines Inventory		₱2,798,430.00
To revert Drugs and Medicines Inventory received on January 2024.
Prepare adjusting journal entry to recognize the distributed and unrecorded textbooks still on hand at year-end as follows:
Textbooks and learning materials Expense		₱918,901.00
Textbooks and learning materials inventory		₱918,901.00
To adjust the net amount of distributed and unrecorded textbooks on-hand at year-end.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting journal entry to correct the misclassified accounts and to reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected. Moreover, in the recording of transactions, the Municipal Accountant shall refer with the description of accounts provided under COA Circular No. 2015-09.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
strictly abide with the generally accepted accounting principles laid down in IPSAS and other COA rules and regulations; and
prepare adjusting journal entry to recognize the fair value or historical cost of the unrecorded projects at Ecopark using the fund transfers from the National Government as well as its related depreciation, as follows:
Building					₱10,855,151.68
Other Structure				₱5,801,130.97

Prior Period Adjustment (previous years’ depreciation)       xxxx
Accumulated Depreciation – Building and Other Structure xxxx
To recognize unrecorded depreciation expenses in 2019 to 2023.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the:
The Municipal Treasurer to:
prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant with a list of unreleased checks at the end of the year. Moreover, proper coordination between the Accounting and Treasurer’s Office should be made to monitor these unreleased checks so that appropriate adjustments can be made in a timely manner; and
stamp all stale checks with a “CANCELLED” mark and practice sending a written notification to the payee regarding the existence of the check at least one month before the check becomes stale.
The Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments for unreleased checks at year-end, as follows:
Cash in Bank - LCCA			  ₱5,337,063.00
Accounts payable				₱5,337,063.00
To revert unreleased checks.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the deficiencies noted by the Audit Team in the presentation of the reconciliation of actual amounts per SCBAA and per SFPer  in the Notes to Financial Statements to enhance the reliability of the Financial Statements of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the:
Municipal Engineer to:
Coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in regularly reconciling the Construction in Progress accounts with ongoing PPAs to accurately reflect the amount and status of the projects in the Quarterly Report on PPAs.
The quarterly Report on PPAs must be accomplished by the Municipal Engineering Office, and not by the Municipal Accounting Office, for it requires technical knowledge on the status/ progress of the PPAs especially infrastructure projects.
Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entry to reclassify the completed electrification of Engred Landfills reported in the CIP account as follows:
Power Supply Systems				 ₱1,000,000.00
CIP – Building &Othe Structure			₱1,000,000.00
To reclassify the completed electrification of Engineered Landfills.
Municipal Accountant to recognize depreciation expense, if the PPAs were completed during CY 2023, or prior period adjustments, if the PPAs were completed in previous years through the following pro-forma entries:
Depreciation Expense – Infrastructure Assets or
Prior Period Adjustment				xxx
Accumulated Depreciation – Power Supply Systems	 xxx
To recognize depreciation on completed projects during CY 2023.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the following:
Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal Treasurer to take into consideration the usual budgeting, and accounting rules and regulations in the implementation of projects, programs, and activities to avoid any legal repercussions;
Municipal Treasurer to pay only expenditures or obligations where the specific fund has been appropriated; and
Municipal Accountant to conduct regular reconciliation of the accounts to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are equal at all times.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
Require the Petty Cash Custodian to:
Be guided with the proper utilization of PCF by ensuring that all transactions threshed out of PCF are not regular expenses, such as the AICS, of the LGU;
Faithfully comply with the provisions of Section 48 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs and COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Refrain from paying expenses that have improperly accomplished and incomplete supporting documents as required in Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and COA Circular 2023-004 dated June 14, 2023.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
ensure the completeness of the supporting documents of the PCVs in accordance with COA Circular 2023-004; and
strictly abide with the pro-forma entries prescribed in Manual on NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the:
Municipal Treasurer to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list of taxpayers, showing their names and the amount due and collectible based on the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card to establish an accurate and reliable basis for setting up of receivables for RPT and SET at the beginning of the year, ensuring the fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
Municipal Accountant to discontinue the practice of closing the previous years’ RPT and SET receivables balance to accurately present the actual amounts due from the taxpayers in the financial statements.
Municipal Treasurer to ensure an accurate and updated reporting of the list of delinquent real properties.
The Treasury Office to:
Conduct an information drive and tax campaign to raise the awareness of its constituents regarding the advance payment of the real property taxes and to avail the discounts, encouraging taxpayers to pay promptly and improving collection efficiency of the Municipality on RPT/SET; and
Continue sending letters to delinquent taxpayers and post notice of delinquency at the main entrance of the municipal hall and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each Barangay of the local government unit concerned. Also, the Municipality may apply the available legal remedies to generate more revenue that may be appropriated for the implementation of additional programs and projects for the benefit of its constituents.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct GSO to relocate the motor oils, and put it in safe storage with proper safeguards, preferably within the premises owned by the Municipal Government to prevent any misuse and unreasonable issuance of the same.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the concerned official to provide a written notice of termination consistent with the provisions of the MOA between the Municipal Government and Mega Station Enterprises and to collect the ₱100,000.00 guaranty deposit from the latter.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Inspection Officer and General Services Officer to submit copies of fully accomplished Inspection and Acceptance Reports to the Office of the Auditor within twenty – four (24) hours after the inspection and acceptance of all consumable deliveries by the said municipal officers. This will allow the Audit team to conduct inspection of deliveries and propose corrective measures when deviations from specifications are detected and thus address the risk of accepting defective goods.

To protect the interest of the Municipal Government from any adverse claim by third parties over the government properties, we recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
Instruct the Municipal Assessor, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, to prioritize the application for transfer and registration of ownership for the fifteen parcels of land with book value of ₱4,429,400.00;
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds to cover all expenses associated with the land registration process; and
Direct the Municipal Assessor, in collaboration with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO, to exhaust all possible means to locate all the documents related to the 15 untitled lots owned by the LGU to facilitate titling of the said real properties.

To ensure that financial assistance granted is valid, regular and reasonable, we recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Formulate policies and guidelines or adopt the policies and guidelines promulgated by DSWD on the grant of financial assistance under the AICS program to strengthen the controls employed in the selection of eligible beneficiaries;
b. Formalize these policies through the issuance of Executive Order, memorandum or equivalent document which will serve as a source of guidance in the implementation of AICs.
c. Ensure that the MSWDO strictly assess and evaluate all applicants requesting financial assistance out of the AICS program including the identification of nearest kin who serves as the claimant; and
d. Require the Municipal Accountant to be more circumspect in reviewing completeness of supporting documents.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to:
Refrain from releasing the shares of the barangay from RPT collections, including interest thereon, in a lump sum. Instead, these shares should be remitted to the Barangays on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the provision of Section 271 (d) of RA No. 7160 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for their operation and PPAs which are beneficial to its constituents; and
Designate another/alternative personnel to process the required documents relative to the timely release of the share of barangays from RPT every quarter.

We reiterated our prior recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and General Services Officer to take necessary means to remove the motto markings made on these heavy equipment/vehicles and on all government properties.

We recommended that Management ensure that Gas Slips/Driver’s Trip Ticket clearly state the purpose or the detailed information on the intended use of the fuel. Also, ensure that the Gas Slips and Driver’s Tickets include plate numbers or vehicle descriptions for proper identification of the vehicles/heavy equipment and to verify that gasoline purchases are made exclusively for authorized government vehicles.
Furthermore, require the concerned personnel issuing gas issue slips to have a complete list of all municipal motor vehicles, authorized drivers/ personnel, and the list of corresponding maximum fuel tank capacity of each vehicle/equipment as a reference in approving the number of liters in the gas issue slip/Driver’s Trip Ticket before authorized driver/personnel can procure gasoline.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to refrain from allowing expenditures which are beyond the approved budget to prevent the incurrence of another overdraft in appropriation;
BAC Chairman/ General Services Officer not to procure and enter into contracts unless there is an appropriation therefor, the unexpended balance of which, free of other obligations, is sufficient to cover the proposed expenditure and
Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant, General Services Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Local Chief Executive to refund the amount disbursed for Fuel, Oil and Lubricants Expenses without covering appropriation, and those gasoline used for privately owned vehicles.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the conduct of regular coordination between the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer, and Municipal Budget Officer to discuss the Status of Appropriation, Allotment, Obligations, and Balances; Statement of Receipts and Expenditures; the Financial Statements; and Bank Reconciliation Statements and to analyze the cash and cash equivalents to determine the financial status of the Municipality in order to prevent budget overdraft and cash deficit.
Also, the Management must limit disbursement within the appropriated budget and specific purpose for which they are appropriated/created and prioritized funding and implementation subject to availability of funds. Lastly, Management must verify that a PPA has appropriation and cash backing before implementation of the same.
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to stop the practice of providing smartphones which is deemed unnecessary and contrary to Section 4 (2) of PD of 1445.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to ensure the judicious utilization of 20% DF and execute the Annual Investment Plan with urgency to prevent the possible loss and wastage of government assets.
Further, we recommended the following:
The Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with concerned Municipal Officers, to ensure that the program of works, project designs, and other documents needing approval had been prepared and approved without objections prior to its inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan;
The MPDC, in coordination with Municipal Engineer, and Municipal Tourism Officer to closely monitor the utilization and implementation of improvement and maintenance of Ecopark; and
The MPDC and Municipal Tourism Officer Designate to conduct studies and make plans, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, to improve the Ecopark to fully maximize the utilization thereof through the creation of various revenue-generating projects and activities in the Ecopark to help fund various government PPAs for the benefit of its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instructs the following officers:
Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO), to coordinate with the DENR and other regulatory agencies as to the permits and requirements to immediately utilize the two (2) unit cells located in the engineered sanitary landfill;
Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with MENRO and Municipal Engineer, to allocate funds to cover all expenses associated with the processing of the permits and requirements by the DENR. Further, ensure that the program of works, project designs, and other documents needing approval have been prepared and approved without objections prior to its inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan; and
After securing the required certification; the Municipal Engineer to immediately utilize the two-unit cells at the sanitary landfill in order to reduce expenses incurred on acquiring services from the private contractor in disposing of wastes outside the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the BAC, through the Secretariat, to post the invitation or request for submission of price quotations/proposals, the notice of award, contract or purchase order, including notice to proceed if necessary, in the PhilGEPS website, the website of the Procuring Entity concerned, if available, and at any conspicuous place for all procurement modalities mentioned in Sections 54.2 and 54.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in order to promote transparency in the procurement activities.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the:
Municipal Engineer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant:
to regularly monitor and validate progress in infrastructure projects to ensure that the construction of projects is executed according to the contract and to accurately reflect the status in the Quarterly Report on PPAs;
Suspend the work, when necessary, in a timely manner through a written order duly received by the contractor in accordance with Section 10 of Annex “E” of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184. Accordingly, indicate the suspension order and adjust the contract time in the Quarterly Report on PPAs; and
Determine the amount of liquidated damages to be imposed to the contractor and demand settlement. When payment cannot be made, deduct the amount from the final payment to the contractor.
BAC Chairman to ensure that all necessary project documents, including the feasibility study, are prepared and that all project plans and documents comply with the requirements or the terms and conditions set forth by the lending institution before awarding the project.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the BAC Secretariat to observe the prescribed periods in the posting of NOA, NTP, and Approved Contract on the website of PhilGEPS.
Additionally, ensure that the certifications on the advertisement of ITB and posting of NOA at any conspicuous place in the premises of the Municipality are performed within the prescribed period of posting

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 on the required supporting documents regarding the advance payment for mobilization cost before processing the claims of the contractors.

1.14. We recommended and Management agreed to observe the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to have reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and accountability as follows:
a. Adopt the uniform numbering system for property numbers of PPE and require the Property Unit to update the property stickers based on the prescribed format and to include the required information under Section 5.6 and 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
b. Temporarily relieve the members of the Inventory Committee of all their regular duties to devote their full time to the conduct of the physical inventory taking; and
c. Strictly follow the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of PPE items found at the station, and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items and require the Accountant and Property Officer to reconcile their records based on the results of the actual physical inventory and effect necessary adjustments.

3.9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Draw the following entry:
Due to LGUs 2,270,970
Due to NGAs 1,227,380.87
Due to GOCCs 1,043,588.93
b. Provide the Municipal Treasurer with the records of unutilized PDAF and DAP so that the latter could immediately return the same to the Bureau of Treasury; and
c. Trace, locate and recover the documents associated with the unreliable balances

4.11. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary accounting entries to transfer from the GF to the Special Trust Fund all the unexpended/unobligated QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE balances amounting to ₱1,574,284.42.

5.10. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Prepare the correcting entries on the fund transfer in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015;
b. Avoid transferring LDRRMF-Capital Outlay to STF; and
c. Submit the SB Resolution authorizing the transfer.

6.9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Maintain subsidiary ledger to substantiate the A/P account;
b. Ensure that obligations incurred are properly recorded in the books of the. Municipality as soon as the goods are accepted or services rendered, and the corresponding bills are received from the contractor or supplier; and
c. Henceforth, strictly comply with the regulation on the proper booking up of obligations according to Section 4(s) of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I, to fairly present the A/P in the financial statements at year-end.

8.6. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully review the transactions and make appropriate adjustments to ensure that the costs are accurately reclassified to the appropriate asset or expense account

9.13. We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Direct the Municipal Assessor-ICO to coordinate with and seek technical assistance from the Provincial Assessor and/or the DOF-Bureau of Local Government Finance to fast-track the preparation of the updated SMVs of the Municipality pursuant to Section 212 of the LGC and cause the drafting of an Ordinance for consideration of the Sanggunian, incorporating therein the proposed SMV, assessment level and tax rate to support the conduct of GRRPA within the reglementary period;
b. Augment the number of qualified personnel assigned at the Municipal Assessor’s Office to meet the minimum personnel requirements stipulated in the MRPAAO and conduct capacity-building interventions to facilitate the conduct of the general revision of real property assessments in adherence to Section 219 of the same Code and DILG-DOF JMC No. 2010-001, and closely monitor its completion; and
c. Make representation with the Local Chief Executive/Local Sanggunian to appropriate funds for the computerization of the assessment records to hasten the completion of GRRPA data gathering and encoding.

10.14. We recommended that Management consider terminating the contract pursuant to Section 9.4.1 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, and Section III.A.2, Annex I of the same IRR.

10.15. We further recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Engineer strictly monitor the implementation of infrastructure projects to ensure adherence to contract timelines and, if necessary, resort to legal remedies provided under the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 such as imposing sanctions on the erring contractor, emphasizing the importance of fulfilling the contractual obligations and completing the project promptly.
11.7. We recommended that Management strictly observe the installation of signboards/billboards in the project’s location in compliance with COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.
12.8. We recommended and Management agreed to stop paying contractors from cash advances and instead issue checks to the respective payees/suppliers.

13.10. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that all cash advances are supported by the complete documentary requirements pursuant to P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001, and immediately disapprove cash advances without the necessary supporting documents.

13.11. We also recommended that Management submit a written explanation of the non-submission/lack of documentary requirements for the cash advances made in CY 2022.

14.9. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant provide disclosures on the face of the SCBAA and/or in the Note to FS the material discrepancies between the budget and actual amounts and provide a reconciliation of actual amounts per SCBAA and per financial statements, to enhance the transparency of the Municipality’s financial statements.

15.6. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant revise the Notes to the Financial Statements to provide the necessary disclosures such as the name of the lending institution, the principal amount, the term, the interest rate, the maturity date, the purpose of the loan, and interest expense of the Loans Payable - Domestic account.

We recommended that the LCE oblige the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the DVs for audit and verification purposes. 

For the DVs affected by the massive flooding brought by Typhoon Maring, require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer in coordination with the Implementing Offices to exert more efforts in the reconstruction of the remaining transaction documents for submission to the Auditor concerned, thus Management will be immediately informed of any deficiencies noted in the audit of the transactions. 

Further, Management strictly observed the timely submission of transaction documents to facilitate the review thereon and avoid further audit action as provided under Section 122 (2) of PD No. 1445.

2. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare a journal entry voucher to rectify the affected accounts.

We further recommended that going forward, the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the provisions outlined in COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, specifically when recording the 15% advance payment to contractors and consequently use appropriate accounts in the recording of payments related to infrastructure projects.

observations.managementAction: Propose a Supplemental Budget taken from the Beginning Balance of the General Fund to replenish the said amount to the 20% DF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer properly identify and assess the hazards susceptibility and the related risk that may occur in the Municipality in order to formulate a feasible LDRRM-related PPAs. We further recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and benefits of the fund, and strictly observe the provisions of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2013-1 in allocating LDRRM Fund solely for the implementation of DRRM-related projects.
observations.managementAction: Management to properly identify and assess hazards and to strive for full implementation.

observations.managementAction: The Municipality had been fully utilized; the Municipal Accountant indicated in AAPSI that the Bayanihan Grant has been fully utilized.

observations.recommendation: Conduct actual Inventory inspection of all inventoriable items of the LGU and determine it it’s active or inactive and apply for write of all inventories that are inactive. Direct (a) the Inventory Committee to conduct a physical count of inventories and movable properties of the Municipality; (b) the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the report on the results of the count using the prescribed form and reconcile the results with the accounting records; (c) the Municipal Treasurer, as property custodian, and the Municipal Accountant to maintain stock/property cards and ledger cards, respectively.
observations.managementAction: Conduct actual Inventory inspection of all inventoriable items in the LGU to perform the actual inventory of all inventoriable items in the LGU and determine it’s active or inactive. Implementation started on December 10, 2024 and January 31, 2025. Two J.O.s were hired to perform the actual inventory. Formulation of PIP ongoing.

observations.recommendation: Make an Adjusting Entry. The Municipal Accountant capitalize all major repairs or improvements of assets in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to present fairly the financial statements of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Accountant to make JEV to verify the corrections. The Municipal Accountant implemented adjustments in the following year to book as asset.

observations.recommendation: Directed the Municipal Accountant to release immediately the barangays’ shares of prior years and succeeding shares on a quarterly basis within five (5) days after the end of each quarter.
observations.managementAction: Distribute the amount to the Barangay Accountant and Treasurer. The municipal Accountant already remitted the said balances and those appearing in the current SL balances are recent RPTs.

observations.recommendation: Directed all those who received the PEI to refund the amount of ₱10,000.00 each, the payment of which is not legally due them, the PS limitation provided under Section 325(a) of RA 7160, having been not observed by the municipality. Henceforth, management shall strictly observe the fifty-five percent (55%) limitation on personal services to prevent disallowances in audit.
observations.managementAction: Effect the Refund and immediate repayment. Repayments had been deducted from payroll. However, there were still unsettled disallowances that belonged to former employees who were either deceased or separated from the service.

observations.recommendation: Direct all persons held liable therefore to immediately and fully settle the disallowances in audit pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 to enable the Municipality to utilize the funds for its priority development projects.
observations.managementAction: Increase the Refund and effect the Refund. The grant of allowance had already been stopped, and partial settlements were already made. However, there were still unsettled disallowances that apparently belonged to former employees who were either deceased or separated from the service.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the Supply Officer designee to furnish the auditor with the approved purchase orders and notify her of the deliveries of supplies within 24 hours from issuance and acceptance, so that surprise and selective inspection of the deliveries be made.
observations.managementAction: Notify COA BAC secretariat upon issuance and acceptance. POs were not timely notified and submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: Initiate appropriate measures for recovery of individual records of borrowers. Intensify the collection of KKK loan repayment by issuing individual bills of collection. Initiate relending of said assistance to qualified applicants duly screened by a committee created for that purpose. Make representation with the local law-making body for a possible write-off accounts deemed uncollectible. Apply for Write Off to COA.
observations.managementAction: Apply for Write Off to COA with resolution submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Inventory Committee to follow the guidelines and procedures as provided for in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of those found at station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of the Municipality to establish the existence, condition and accuracy of PPE account balances. For the missing PPE items, require the end-user or accountable officer concerned to submit immediately to the Auditor concerned an application for request for relief from property accountability duly supported with the requisite documents as provided under COA Memorandum No. 92-751 dated February 24, 1992 to facilitate or ensure its evaluation and resolution.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee was not able to conduct the one-time cleansing of PPE.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 1, pages 40 to 42 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of inventories semestrally and submit the corresponding report to the Auditor not later July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively, for verification. Also, require the Supply Officer to coordinate with the implementing offices/ Department Heads for the submission of the RIS and Certified List of Recipients to the Municipal Accountant as her basis in recording the issuances of inventories to the end-users in order to present the correct balances of the inventory accounts.
observations.managementAction: Conducted physical count of inventories, however, reconciliation with the accounting records was not made.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 5, pages 49 to 52 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE oblige the concerned officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances to submit immediately the liquidation report and supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books of accounts and submit the same to the Auditor concerned for audit and verification purposes; failure to comply, consider the imposition of sanctions such as the withholding of their salaries as provided for in Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002. We further recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances in order to prevent accumulation of unliquidated cash advances and the possible loss or misuse of government funds.
observations.managementAction: Cash advances totaling ₱85,200.00 were liquidated during the year.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 4, pages 47 to 49 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the concerned offices to take appropriate actions to enforce the collection of the overdue loans. We further recommended that Management remain resolute in implementing the provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement especially the penalty clause to avoid the continuing accumulation of outstanding receivables. Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for impairment on the estimated percentage of receivable that were expected to be uncollectible in order to fairly present affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management was not able to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: No collection of the overdue loans was made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE oblige the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the DVs including the cancelled checks for audit and verification purposes. For the DVs affected by the massive flooding brought by Typhoon Maring, require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer in coordination with the Implementing Offices to exert best efforts in the reconstruction of the transaction documents for submission to the Auditor concerned, thus Management will be immediately informed of any deficiencies noted in the audit of the transactions. Further, Management strictly observe the timely submission of transaction documents to facilitate the review thereon and avoid further audit action as provided under Section 122 (2) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Two Hundred Twenty-Three (223) DVs were submitted for audit.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 8, pages 59 to 61 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE oblige the BAC to require from the winning bidders the submission of the CHSP duly approved by the DOLE which shall form part of the contracts for the implementation of infrastructure projects to ensure the protection and welfare of workers and the general public within and around the immediate vicinity of the construction worksite.
observations.managementAction: The approval of CHSP of some projects was made beyond the completion of the projects.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 15, pages 77 to 79 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Implementing Offices to formulate their respective PPMPs showing the specific program/ project/ activity to be implemented and provide the necessary information needed to facilitate the consolidation of the same into APP to ensure transparency and provide a good basis for monitoring the implementation of the procurement activities.
observations.managementAction: The Consolidated APP for CY 2022 still did not contain the specific PPA to be implemented
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 10, pages 65 to 67 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Treasurer to verify the completeness of the RCD of each Collector by checking the statement of accountable forms as to initial balances on hand, receipts, issues and the ending balances on hand and make a physical count of the accountable forms remaining in the custody of the collector/ teller to ensure that the accountable forms under the custody of the collectors and collections received are intact. Moreover, require the Municipal Treasurer to consolidate monthly the individual RAAF of the accountable officers and submit the same to the Auditor Concerned to facilitate the verification of accountability for accountable forms under their custody, thus any discrepancy will be communicated to Management for immediate and appropriate action.
observations.managementAction: Monthly CRAAF was not submitted for verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to comply with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 regarding the submission of copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders with their corresponding supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) working days from execution and issuance thereof for review and evaluation so that management will be immediately informed of any defects and deficiencies noted and to facilitate the inspection of completed projects and delivered of goods.
observations.managementAction: The BAC submitted copies of contracts for review purposes.
observations.reason: Submission was not made within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the concerned implementing offices the prompt implementation of all projects/ programs funded out of the tobacco excise tax in order to give the constituents, especially the tobacco farmers the early benefits that could be derived therefrom. We further recommended that the LCE advise the Local Development Council and Municipal Accountant to determine the total unexpended balances of the completed and unimplemented projects and inform the LCE for possible reappropriation and the latter to request Sangguniang Bayan to prepare resolution for the reappropriation of the unused funds in accordance with the authorized uses as prescribed in RA Nos. 7171 and 8240 and ensure that only feasible programs/ projects/ activities are included in the Annual Investment Program and require regular monitoring of implementation as basis in taking appropriate action.
observations.managementAction: Various projects were still not implemented during the year.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 19, pages 85 to 87 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the implementing offices to coordinate and strategize the implementation of all the programs/ projects embodied under the 20% Development Fund to attain desirable socio-economic targets and outcomes for the Municipality and intended beneficiaries. Moreover, require them to review the composition of the 20% Development Fund, identify the savings from fully implemented projects and those which are no longer prioritized and, subject to the provisions of the Local Government Code and the DILG-DBM JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, reappropriate to current/ existing priority development projects.
observations.managementAction: Programs and projects with appropriation amounting to ₱51,340,995.01 which represents 71.97 percent of the total amount of ₱71,333.049.18 were not implemented.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 17, pages 81 to 82 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the GAD Focal Person to formulate feasible programs/ projects/ activities in order to fully address gender issues/ problems of the constituents in the municipal level and to help the national government attain its goal of addressing gender issues in the country.
observations.managementAction: Some programs and projects during the year were not fully implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Inventory Committee to follow the guidelines and procedures as provided for in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of those found at station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of the Municipality to establish the existence, condition and accuracy of PPE account balances as various PPE items were affected during the onslaught of Typhoon Maring in CY 2021. This will facilitate the reconciliation of records with the Municipal Accounting Office and the preparation of necessary accounting entries.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the depository banks to obtain the necessary record or information that would support the identified reconciling items for recording in the books of accounts. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to take up the cancellation of the stale checks. We also recommended to require the Municipal Treasurer, that at least one month before a check becomes stale, send a written notice to the payee of the existence of the check to facilitate the replacement thereof.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to exert more efforts to locate any possible documents which could provide the details as to the debtors, nature and purpose of funds. Also, require the provision of allowance for impairment on the dormant and unsubstantiated receivables as provided under IPSAS 29 to present its correct net realizable value. After which, comply with the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the request for write-off of the identified dormant and unsubstantiated receivables, thus receivable accounts would be fairly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to demand the concerned officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances to submit immediately the liquidation report and supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books of accounts and submit the same to the Auditor concerned for audit and verification purposes, otherwise, consider the imposition of sanctions such as the withholding of their salaries as provided for in Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
We further recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances in order to prevent accumulation of unliquidated cash advances and the possible loss or misuse of government funds.

We recommended that the LCE require the Supply Officer to coordinate with the implementing offices for the submission of the RIS and Certified List of Recipients to the Municipal Accountant as her basis in recording the issuances of inventories to the end-users Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer to reconcile their records and adjust them based on valid documents in order to present the correct balances of the inventory accounts and the related accounts.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to determine all tangible items which were recognized as PPE that do not meet the capitalization threshold and reclassify the same to the appropriate semi-expendable inventory account if not yet issued or close it to the Government Equity account if already issued to the end-user. Moreover, require the Municipal Budget Officer to see to it that appropriation for Capital Outlay is in accordance with the specified capitalization threshold for proper recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the LCE require:
The Municipal Accountant to verify the validity of payables aged two years and more and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors. Claims with valid supporting documents shall be paid. Otherwise, cause the reversion of undocumented accounts payables to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund. Moreover, return/remit the unused/unexpended balances of completed programs and projects funded by various national government agencies based on the stipulation indicated in the Memorandum of Agreement/Trust Agreements. Lastly, analyze the existence of negative subsidiary ledger balances and make the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts to correct the affected accounts.
Require the Municipal Treasury Office to maintain Real Property Tax Account Register to identify the list of taxpayers and uncollected amounts that would support the established deferred income from real property and special education taxes in previous years.

We recommended that the LCE oblige the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the DVs for audit and verification purposes. For the DVs affected by the massive flooding brought by Typhoon Maring, require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer in coordination with the Implementing Offices to exert more efforts in the reconstruction of the remaining transaction documents for submission to the Auditor concerned, thus Management will be immediately informed of any deficiencies noted in the audit of the transactions. Further, Management strictly observes the timely submission of transaction documents to facilitate the review thereon and avoid further audit action as provided under Section 122 (2) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents and justifications on the deficiencies observed for further evaluation of the transactions and to avoid suspension in audit. Also, oblige the Municipal Accountant see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with the required basic documents to ensure their validity and propriety.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Department Heads concerned to formulate their respective PPMPs showing the specific procurement program/project to be implemented and provide the necessary information needed to facilitate the consolidation of the same into APP to ensure transparency and provide an accurate basis for monitoring the implementation of the procurement activities.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to reiterate our demand to the Former Municipal Treasurer for the immediate restitution of the missing funds. Also, require her to present the missing accountable forms, thus her total accountability could be fully established.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to subject all procurement to public bidding unless the conditions are present for the use of alternative methods upon approval of the LCE to ensure that the most advantageous price and terms for the LGU is obtained.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to adopt the required procedures and conditions in the verification and validation of all documents submitted by the bidders/suppliers to ensure that the Municipal Mayor being the Head of the Procuring Entity is entering into a contract with a technically, legally and financially capable supplier to promote competitiveness in the procurement activities and avoid future disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Implementing Office/s to conduct market research during the procurement planning stage to ensure that relevant factors are considered in determining the cost and quantity of items to be included in the PPMP. Require submission of a revised PPMP for any change thereto and submit to the BAC, through its Secretariat, for consolidation and approval of the Local Chief Executive.

We recommended that the LCE oblige either the BAC or the Municipal Engineer to monitor the submission of the CSHP duly approved by the DOLE from the winning bidders before the signing of contract to ensure the protection and welfare of workers and the general public within and around the immediate vicinity of the construction worksite. Also, require the Municipal Accountant to see to it that payments of infrastructure projects are supported with valid Certificate of CSHP Approval by the DOLE to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer to maintain Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer's Index Card to identify the list of taxpayers and uncollected amounts that would support the established receivables from real property taxes and to monitor tax collections and delinquencies.
